name: James Baily
clubs: Undergraduate Member of Alumni Reunion Committee, (S.)
Executive Committee Y. M. C. A., (S.)
description: Ladies and gentlemen—The first person on our program is the pious and perfect James Baily. "But I thought he led a fast life at Hopkins," exclaimed someone in the audience. "Oh, no, madam," said Pat, "he was merely fleeing from temptation when you saw him. That's the way the track coaches save whiskey; they just show Jim a drink and he does the 440 in 50 flat. Then they give the whiskey to the rest of the team, who proceed to do the 440 in 49.4." Jim has a rather long list of honors, all gained by fleeing from temptation. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Isidor Blum
description: We had a hard time getting this picture, ladies and gentlemen. Blum is trying to solve the perpetual motion problem in his own person. For this reason, and because of the peculiar type of his beauty, we had to make nine different and separate attempts before we secured a portrait that was worthy of a place in this series of beauties. Blum is going to study law, because he wants to be a social and political reformer. He has, at various times, advocated asset currency, free trade, woman suffrage, and other revolutionary measures, besides suggesting many improvements (?) in the University. Busy as he always is—or, rather seems—he is ever ready for an argument, especially an economic argument. Let's change the subject, Larry.

name: William Lumsdon Bond
description: The quiet, unobtrusive person who is now depicted on the screen is the far-famed silent member of our class. Billy says little, but he thinks much. Just what he thinks I'd hate to tell you. If any of the gentlemen wish to hear Billy's stories of what he used to do "down in Virginia," he will be glad to tell you. He is composing a book called "How to Bluff Hulburt." Billy is a runner, too. He trains by smoking a double quantity of cigarettes. He has been known to win a race. How he did it no one knows—least of all, Billy. Rumor has it that he was too excited to get tired. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Chew, Samuel C., Jr.
description: Ladies and gentlemen---here we have the only original spring poet. Sixteen sonnets per day is his record; writes two copies, sends one to the NEWS-LETTER and one to the lady. "What lady?" asked the pale-faced damsel, with the four false curls, with true feminine curiosity. "Ah! Miss, that's too much for me; they are widely scattered." Reasonably respectable, though he is in Baltimore, at one time in his checkered career he hied himself to Paris and remained hidden from the world for many moons. Turn the crank, Larry. "Wait!" cried the enthusiastic sheath-gown girl, "I want to see how he parts his hair!" "Hurry up, Larry," said Pat, "the ladies are in a hurry to see George Corner."

name: Corner, George Washington
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Recording Secretary, (J.)
description: Ah, here he is ladies—the redoubtable George Washington, the second—I mean third. From his doleful countenance and disordered appearance you will easily realize that this is our class poet. "Poet?" asked the long-haired naturalist in a doubtful tone. "Prezactly, sir; 'tis a budding genius—though some say there's a worm in the bud—still he will guarantee a production of 7,286 gallons of verse per day, and is universally celebrated for his ability to turn in enough 'copy' for the NEWS-LETTER at a moment's notice—anyone can see that it's written in half a minute." Whizz the crank, Larry.

name: Harry Vail Deale, II. K. X.
description: I'll read you one of my own poems about this gentleman, ladies: This is a sucker named Deale, Who emits a silvery squeal, When he gets a good square meal. He went to Psychology one day, Doc. Watson got a little bit gay, And made Buddie feel like a jay. Poor Buddie went home feeling bad, Calling Watson a grumbling "grad," Which made monkey-trainer damn mad. Hello, there! What d'ye turn the crank for, Larry?

name: Allan Herbert Fisher
motto: "What's that?" asked the long-haired man with the intellectual brow. "Chaucer, of course, sir, who wants to know that damned rot?"
description: This runt could give points to the Standard Oil Co. if he wanted to, and is the smartest little devil (after five hours of study) you ever saw. He sleeps in Barnett's bookcase, shines Ballagh's shoes, and presses French's trousers. What Richard don't know ain't worth knowing. He studies eighteen days before each exam; bootlicks twenty-three hours a day, and struts around, proud as a peacock, but not half so beautiful. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Samuel Fredman
description: Now, my good people, don't get excited. This is not one of Watson's pets. "He looks like them, anyway," piped Pete, from the gallery. Now don't be unkind, my dear sir. That foolish expression comes from listening to Kirby Smith's talks on the evils of all drinks but water. This harmless creature was captured in the wilds of Roanoke, Va., and brought to this city some years ago. Nevertheless, he does not look any more civilized now than he did then. We are sorry for him, in one way, for he's been so smitten with the charms of one of the fair ones that he—well, all right, if you fellows in the gallery insist, we'll pass on to the next.

name: Frey, Henry Warren
description: My mother and father are Irish—yes, they are. Did you ever hear Frey talk? Say, it's awful. Talk is Frey's strong point. He talked himself through rhetoric, talked himself into third assistant sub. on the class football team, talked himself into vice-president and expects to talk himself into the presidency of the Highlandtown village council. "Oh, I know what he is; he's a hot-air blower," said a wise-looking guy in a smiling checkered suit. "You said it, sir, that's what he is." When it comes to telling you how to talk your way through Uncle Henry Wood's Faust course, Frey can bellyache all day. His name isn't Frey, it's Wind. Blow him away, Larry.

name: Edward Percy Guerard, Jr.
clubs: Winner Freshman Speaking Contest, Adams Medal
Blue Numerals, Member of Cast, Haresfoot Club
description: Ladies and gentlemen—This is it. “What’s it?” says the old lady. Why, this is it. It’s a cynic. By dint of frequent burning of the midnight oil, this has evolved a system of philosophy which baffles all attempts at analyzation. It makes him refuse even to buy text-books until a week or so before exams; yet it enables him to pass with marks above those of many a grind. It enables him to deliver speeches that bring tears to the eyes of old ladies, but make Dr. French blush from mortification. In the newspaper, on one occasion, he was called the “silver-tongued orator of the South,” and yet he doesn’t debate on the ’Varsity team. There is but one fault to be found with his philosophy. He is cynical to an extreme degree, especially where femininity is concerned. He has a supreme contempt, outwardly at least, for fussing and fussers. And yet, rumors from the far South, seem to show that he, too, is a prey to the human emotions and passions.

name: Gutman, Leo J.
motto: "Here," said Pat, "we have the greatest lacrosse player. For him lacrosse was the most important subject on the Hopkins curriculum. Following the Faculty's rule of pursuing at least one subject for three years, Gutman chose lacrosse. 'Tis true, then, that he paid attention to a few other studies—for example, track athletics. He also took the Freshman and Junior courses in football, but lacrosse was his major." "He looks like the chap who soaked me for an ad. in the HULLABALOO," said the baldheaded gentleman in the orchestra. "You're right, my friend," said Pat, "but let's forgive him for that." Shove in the next, Larry.

name: Hardcastle, Harry K.
description: Here, ladies and gentlemen, we have the giggling member from the wild and woolly limestone country. His audible smile is one of the ornaments of Psychology I. He is the only man who was ever requested to reconsider his resignation from the NEWSLETTER. "Oh, he's one of those people who do their work and then beat it, is he?" cried the fusser in the box-seat. "Naw," said Pat, "he just beats it." Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Albert Boucsein Haupt
description: Just for a moment, ladies and the rest, we beg of you to look at this picture of hopelessness, this image of despair, this statue of grief. No sun ever shone for him. Clouds of forebodings forever dimmed his sight. The clouds were especially thick about examination time. It's no wonder though. It would be hard for one to spend three years in the same building with Ames, Bliss and Hulbert, and, after that torture, smile. Poor fellow, he's more to be pitied than censured. Let's have the next, Larry.

name: Oliver Baker Hopkins
description: Here, my dear people, we have one of the handsomest specimens in our collection. This is a picture of the champion quarryman and rock-breaker of the world. But not only did he shine among the rocks and fossils of past ages, but in the world of today; whether out on the lacrosse field or in the halls of debate, whether rushing his opponents towards the goal posts with thousands watching him or rushing the fair sex with not a soul in sight, this son of Virginia always held his own. Notwithstanding his physical ability, however, he has fallen a victim to one of our weaker sisters. "And he so young. It's a pity," sighed the old gray-haired lady in the orchestra. "Ain't it awful, Mabel?" said Pat, as Larry withdrew the slide.

name: Johnson, Walter Vincent
description: Ladies and gentlemen—The aesthetic nature of our subject shines forth very clearly in the picture we present. This is the class artist, and an artist he is to his fingertips. To his indomitable energy and happy faculty of copying appropriate pictures from the newspapers may be attributed the wonderful standard of the illustrations in this book. Nor does his talent stop here. He is an artist in other lines. With true artistic insight, he can pick examination questions with the same unfailing success with which "Hooly" Black picks the losers at Pimlico. His history notes are the delight of his friends and the despair of less fortunate individuals. But, unfortunately, he hides all his talents under the pint-cup of a retiring disposition, which makes us see his blooming countenance in the art gallery only on rare occasions. Hence, we allow you to drink your fill of his beauty now. When all are satiated you may turn the crank, Larry.

name: Robert Lowndes
description: Behold the man of mystery. Nobody knows what Bob does with his time. To be sure, he does play lacrosse, once in a while, and plays it well. And, sometimes, upon rare but remembered occasions, he allows himself to be coaxed into harmonizing with the bunch. But when no one is looking he slips away, and is gone. Next day if you ask him where he has been, he answers you in a vague way that leaves you with the impression that he is a hard student. But we wonder if he really studies. Some think it's a girl. But we can't find her. He is apparently proof against the blandishments of the most accomplished of the other sex. The rest of the students like him, what they see of him, but he never lets himself become entirely popular. He has all the graces that go to make a good fusser, all those that go to make a good fellow, all those that go to make a good student. Yet he is all of these in but a measure. Turn the crank, Larry, lest we be tempted to ponder longer on this problem.

name: Martin, William Hennick
clubs: Corresponding Secretary J. H. U. A. A.
description: As to the picture which we now have before us, I would like to say that it is the prize mug of our collection, representing as it does Billy H. Martin, student and athlete, for Billy has been distinguished in both these fields. He has pulled down several honors in the halls of learning, as well as earning recognition on the lacrosse and football fields. His fortune is his face and in his one notable failing he is in the good company of Joe Ames. Lately we have had some fears for Billy as he has been seen in company with the Candy Kid on the trail of some skirt. But that shows Billy's wisdom. He's a wise guy. Give us the next, Larry.

name: William Howard Michael
motto: "This is the hippopotamus Cupid. 'Hyppo' means 'water' and 'potimus' most probably means 'hater,' for Cupid is a hater of water. Other liquids he tolerates. He is remarkable in many ways. He wanders around in pumps, which, when he is not using, he lends to the harbor-master, who employs them as scows. Just look at them. The greatest treat of all is to see Cupid in the shower bath—truly a wonderful sight. Give us another slide, Larry, and be careful of Cupid—he's delicate."

name: Herbert Levy Moses
description: Under long, shaggy locks, we behold the noble brow of the prophet, Moses. Arouse him not to anger, lest he pour forth his wrath upon you. This pessimist is a chronic kicker. He has a grievance against the whole world—aye, he is not even satisfied with himself. A member of the classical department one day found some curiously marked pages in the basement. Several professors puzzled for hours over what appeared to be some cryptographic manuscript, but could make of it neither heads nor tails. Their relief was mixed with disappointment when they found that the leaves contained merely some of Moses' philosophy notes. You must have heard how, in the debate, he saved the day—or rather the night—for the Seniors. The debate was half over when he left the hall. As the discussion progressed, his speakers looked anxiously toward the door. At last he reappeared, holding a dripping pitcher aloft. The night was won! Victory was ours! The last speaker took a cool and refreshing draught, and arose to complete the deadly work.

name: J. Newman Numsen, K. A. Baltimore.
description: This large specimen is well-nigh as great a stranger to us as he is to you, ladies. He has *just* arrived from his native land—where that is Mike only knows. On first inspection he has appeared to us a decided addition to our galaxy. Every line of his face betokens amiability. He has never ruffled anyone's temper, not even Doc. Watson's. If you want to see him yourself come to Walter's barber shop, and there you will find his along with the other mugs. Next!

name: Onion, George Franklin
description: Here, ladies and gentlemen, we have an excellent representation of the genuine original Candy Kid. "What's all that disturbance at the door, Mike?" "Marshal Farnan wants to come in, sir." "This ain't Salome," says Pat. "Oh, you kid!" exclaimed the playful peroxide lady. "And why the cheerful smile upon his face?" "Been to see his wife," says Pat. "And why the happy grin?" "His wife was out." "And why the tickled look?" "Picked up a pretty little girl on the way home. Turn the crank, Larry. Good evening, Marshal Farnan."

name: Overbeck, Robert Milton
motto: "May I ask," said the greasy grind in the gallery, "which consideration is of more importance to Professor Brush?" "What is it ye're saying? What ye take Murray for? Of course, an auto's more important than a blooming French lesson! But Bob's a fine fellow in spite of his associates, and it is said that he studies those lessons he knows so well while he's waiting for his dom car to start. Turn the crank, Larry."
clubs: Second Vice-President Y. M. C. A. (S.)
description: Ah, ladies, here is a man!—the friend of Murray Brush on two counts—good in French and owner of a motor-car.

name: James Hamilton Owens, J. $\Phi$.
clubs: Recording Secretary Y. M. C. A., (J.)
description: This picture is, ladies and gentlemen, is—oh! young ladies, don't blush; yes, I see you have guessed! He is a most accomplished man, a student, football player, actor and greatest of all fusser—a most persistent one. Indeed, to such an extreme did he carry his fussing that a few months ago the Dean felt called upon to call him to his office and give him a bit of fatherly advice, to the effect that if he wished to continue a member of the University he would have to curtail his fussing. Why, his 'phone bill alone was $12.00 last month, and the report is that in one month he made twenty-three trips to Pikesville. "Oh," says the old lady, "to see the dairy?" "Not at all, madam, to see the dairymaid." Turn the crank quick, Larry, before they break the machine.

name: William Edward Pearce, Jr.
description: The tall blonde-haired individual now upon the canvas is Mr. Pearce. "But why the woe-begone look?" asked the romantic-looking maiden, with the conspicuous rat. "That, miss, is the result of managing the Hullabaloo, which was no easy job for a man in Group I. When anything went wrong either he cussed Chew out, and so got tired, or else Chew cussed him out, which made him more tired. Hence the weary, care-worn expression. Besides nearly every storekeeper is his enemy." "Why?" asked the fat groceryman. "Because he got an ad from nearly every storekeeper. In intervals of getting ads he has become quite an expert in that favorite recreation of the Y. M. C. A., and his pleasant, though anxious face, is often seen in Levering Hall, looking at the queens on the chessboard--- or on Eutaw Street.

name: Franklin Burche Pedrick
clubs: Board of Governors of the Cotillion Club, (J.), (S.)
description: Ladies and gentlemen—here we have the original and only Bingo Boy, captured on the wing among the jungles of Washington. Excellent in his dissecting apron, charming in his Sunday suit, entrancing in full dress! The only specimen in captivity who can turn in his Dean's essay three months late and live to tell the tale. Freshmen are often heard to enquire about something which was and is not; in other words, they have heard of that which in the dim past was known as the Musical Clubs, and they ask what has become of them. Ladies and gentlemen, you are now gazing at the Musical Clubs! When the Musical Clubs (alias F. B. Pedrick) began to take exp. psych. the music was driven from the soul. A bas la psychologie!

name: Russell, Harold Louis
motto: "I rise on a point of order. 'Tis slander to compare me with Daniel Webster."
clubs: Chairman Membership Committee Y. M. C. A. (S.)
Delegate Y. M. C. A. Convention (S.)
Chess Club Winner on Minor Team (F.)
First Undergraduate to do regular work at Homewood
description: Now, ladies and gentlemen, we have here a picture of the famous Daniel Webster. But, hold—I have made a mistake. It can't be so, for these features are a trifle too severe. Yes, I was mistaken; this is not Daniel Webster, but the redoubtable Harold Russell, always ready to lend a helping hand—helping voice, rather—to those who wander from the path of Roberts. He is the modern Daniel Webster. Suddenly the severe lips of the picture opened, and from them issued in solemn tones the following words: "I rise on a point of order. 'Tis slander to compare me with Daniel Webster." "My," tittered the tremulous twins, "we didn't know that these pictures talked." "Well, this one does! Turn the crank, Larry and squelch the phonograph."

name: Frank Saiontz
description: Don't shrink, ladies, it won't hurt you. When President Remsen came to his name at the matriculation exercises, he hesitated. "Saiontz," called the class, when he said, "Here's a name I can't pronounce." "Well," remarked the President, "that's a new kind of science for me." It was. It was a new one on the University. I could talk for hours on this remarkable specimen; and an extended harangue—especially if you didn't catch the drift of my speech—would really give you a good idea of what it means to know Frank. He has something to say at every lecture that he attends. Loquacity, however, is hardly a distinguishing mark at Hopkins. The beauty—pardon me, miss, I mean the peculiarity—of Saiontz is that he always contradicts the lecturer, and always thinks he wins in the ensuing argument. Only once did he ever agree. Dr. French, in the course of a lecture, remarked that many people believe squirrels lay up more nuts before a hard winter. "What would you say about that, Mr. Saiontz?" he asked. "Yes, sir," answered Saiontz. "Yes, what?" "I think squirrels lay more eggs in a hard winter." Wash that egg off the screen, Larry, before you turn on the next film.

name: Schneeberger, Philip
description: The human peacock, ladies. Of particular note is the graceful angle at which he carries what purports to be his head. Given as he is to overzeal in his studies, he proved a willing and officious assistant to Doc. Watson at the time when the latter's celebrated moving picture show was on exhibition in McCoy Hall. Some of the Freshmen took him for the whole show—low vaudeville, of course. He himself thinks he's quite the whole show—a miracle play, I believe. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: F. Janney Smith
clubs: Board of Governors of Cotillion Club, (J.), (S.)
Chairman Board of Governors of Cotillion Club, (S.)
description: Here is the ever smiling, ever jovial, ever loafing Janney—excellent in the barber-shop, radiant at a Hopkins dance, quiet in the classroom (so he won't attract the instructor's attention and get called on). His disposition is his best point, and for that reason he has often had to do the hard jobs on many and various committees. In earlier days he went in a bit for athletics and developed a fine pair of lungs, which he has since used to urge people to come to the Hopkins dances. Let's all come and then Janney's smile will be even broader than it is now. O, you kid!

name: Warren Adams Stewart
description: Say, ladies, ain't he the devil? Why he left home one evening and didn't get back till half past nine. He went down to the Gayety and saw the beautiful ladies pass in the stage entrance, and ever since he's been telling the boys the sights of the demi-monde. When he walks his head comes around the corner a minute or two before the rest of his anatomy—an anatomy, by the way, which nowadays he likes to show in the window of the Hopkins Club, which he joined the moment the Seniors were elected. In this window he stands ogling the Howard Street girls and trying to fancy how it would feel to stand in a big window on the southeast corner of Charles and Eager Streets. Do hurry that next film, Larry!

name: Martillus H. Todd
description: Ladies and gentlemen—We are now gazing at the physiognomy of the Class Baby. Apparently, he was once still younger, as you may see by referring to the other portrait. As he is so young the boys politely let him get better marks than most of them, and then called him a grind. "How terrible," said an old lady in the audience; "has he always been that way?" "Not so," said Pat, "it is the sad effect of delirium tremens, caused by drinking too much milk procured at the University lunchroom." Turn the crank, Larry.

name: August Vogeler
description: Here, ladies and gentlemen, we have what was fondly expected to be the drawing card of this entertainment. Alas! Alas! He has gone into a consumption. "Consumption of what?" asked the meek-eyed maiden. "Consumption of cigarettes," says Pat. "Can't he draw corks?" asked an inquisitive interloper. "Yes, yes," cried Pat, "but he won't even draw fleas for the HULLABALOO." Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Weems, Benjamin Francis
description: This picture, ladies and gentlemen, is BENNIE BUNNY, the cave-dweller. Everything that was, is, or ever shall be, like Chaucer, he knows. This quiet youth of Texan gait is our hope, delight. Seniors, on him alone—and me—rests '09's hope of everlasting fame. Like the —— of Martial, he is the actor par excellence, and those who saw him take the part of Agatha—in "The Magistrate"—can never, never forget it, at least those who were with him behind the scenes never can. Give up all hope, those whom he beguiles into serious conversation, for, like his fame, he is everlasting. Stop! look! listen! Lo, he is about to speak. Turn the crank quick; hurry, Larry, and cork him up!

name: Whitcraft, Franklin Pierce
description: Ladies and gentlemen—you see before you a youth shy and retiring, a lad who chews his cud of gum, swallows it and chews it over again. And, say, he's *awful* scared of the women. He lives out at Lutherville to get out of their way. He "plays lacrosse and shoots for goal"—in vain. When it comes to sheath gowns, merry widows and Anna Held, though—say, ladies, look for him in the baldheaded row. "Who's Anna Held?" asked a callow youth with flaxen hair. Anna Held! Ask any married man. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: William Zebulon White
description: Hello, Larry, what's the matter with that machine? Hurry up that picture! Begorra, Pat, the dom thing won't go in; it's too long! Aw, cut off his feet or his head—it don't make no difference which—and shove it in. Ah! there it is, and I'm sure the ladies won't regret the little delay. Which did you cut off, Larry, his head or his feet? Why his feet, ye dom fool! Oh, yes, on closer look I see that's a face. There's really nothing to add about him—he speaks for himself. Listen and you can hear him! How loud he is! S-sh! Now that's enough, so turn the crank, Larry.

name: George William Bishop
description: Ladies and gentlemen—Here we present to you the bishop of the Class of 1910. Ever since childhood he has frequented the Y. M. C. A., and he is a leader in his line. "What's his line?" asked an old maid on the second row. "Flirting on the John Street line, madam," whispered Pat. "If you ever want to see him, go to the chemical lab, and in the corner where the fumes are thickest and the smell worst you'll find him." Pat was going to say more, but at this point the whole audience shouted, "Turn the crank, Larry!"

name: Otis Herbert Draper
description: Ladies and gentlemen—As you gaze upon this picture you may have some natural curiosity to know why it is included in this remarkable galaxy of objects of interest. This is, ladies and gentlemen, a combination of Methodist preacher and class treasurer. What, you don't believe it! Let me state right here that every statement made in this lecture is the very cream of truth. Did I overhear someone say "curdled?" Kick him out, Larry. To return to our subject, Draper is the only treasurer who has ever been able to get the second half-year's class dues out of—some members. All Methodists are good at lifting collections. Pass around the hat—no, I mean crank, Larry.

name: Ennis, Daniel Lepson
description: Ladies and gentlemen—here we have the only member of this congregation whose feet are set upon the rock of salvation. "Glory, Hallelujah!" exclaimed a pious old lady in the front pew, "Oh, no, I was not referring to his ministerial position, madam; I mean that he is married." "Oh, fudge!" says the old lady. At this juncture someone turned on the hose. "Sop up the water, will you please, Larry," said Pat. He thought it was a fire. Don't let's have a panic, ladies, we'll retint his hair before the next exhibition. Turn the crank, Larry.

name: Raymond Fondersmith Gable
motto: "That’s plenty, Pat," said the girl under the coal scuttle, "let’s turn the crank."
description: Ladies and gentlemen—This picture represents a great deal of labor on the part of our photographer. It was like hunting the shy and elusive willy bird. No one ever sees him after Latin III lets out. It is reported he takes the first train for York and mamma. He is said to have attended the University of Pennsylvania for a year. Though I would like to say more, ladies, there is nothing more to be said.

name: Larrimore, Robert Matthews
description: Say, if this beauty were here he would smile and say to you, very confidentially: "Say, kid, you want to meet a 'queen'?" When you bite and agree, he takes you out somewhere near Catonsville, spends the evening in reminiscences with the girl while you try to look polite and interested. On the way home, he says: "Well, kid, wasn't she the 'queen'?" One night he took two fellows to meet another "queen" at a church festival. They met the "queen", all right, but their sentiments on the noble work being done at the festival have never been repeated in public society. An account of stock disclosed the sad fact that the sum total of their combined capital was eight cents. They parted in disgust without any liquid refreshment. "Robert" is going to be a minister! Taa, taa, Larry, turn the crank.

name: Carl Henry Levan
clubs: Chairman Reception Committee Interclass and Intercollegiate Debate, (S.)
description: My courteous and appreciative audience (jeers from the rear), here is a combination of pie, sauerkraut and dumbness. Anybody who thinks he is Irish—'scuse me, Larry—just refer to Karrelmeyer's Directory of Lebanon, Pa. Short in stature, short in money, short in tobacco, this luckless, devil-may-care youth spends his time equally between kidding Walter, the owner of Walter's Art Gallery, and Edna, the beautiful, golden hair exchange girl. This model young man—quick, turn the crank, Larry.

name: Norman Clyde Marvel
description: Here is another of our beauty squad, ladies and gentlemen—this is Norman Clyde, the marvellous boy. He has tried many things in his time. He once rushed the cane. Since then he has rushed other things, such as girls. I believe he has never been fond of rushing the duck. His prime quality is his voice. Through much practice, in his Freshman year on the Glee Club, he got it so well trained that he has ever been a star debater and is a champion hot-air artist. Quick, Larry, turn the crank before he begins to talk.

name: Francis Key Murray
description: This picture, my dear ladies and gentlemen, represents a most remarkable paradox—he is, in the words of the Dean, on the egotistic side a pessimist, on the altruistic an optimist. In other words, for those who have not been so fortunate as to hear the dear Dean lecture, we will elucidate further by saying that when an affair concerns F. Key alone, he always thinks that the worst is about to happen; but when it concerns one of his friends, he always sees the outcome in a bright and rosy light. This pesso-optimist finds three pleasures in life—horses, hounds and harmony. This trinity of his he loves with a love that is more than love, and he is never so happy as when engaged in the pursuit of one of them. In the morning in the basement of McCoy Hall, or of an evening when he can gather his select band of "harmonizers" about him, he leaps at one bound from this mundane existence to the realms of the skylark. Then, too, we all have often heard how he has pursued the luckless fox across the green fields of merry England, or the hills and dales of Howard County. Gaze once more, ladies and gentlemen, upon his beaming countenance before the crank is turned.

name: Robinson, Aaron
description: Ah! Ah! Aaron Robinson, Esquire. Ah! The wit, the sage, the genius. We understand that Aaron has a reputation for learning and wisdom among his friends. To anybody who really knows him, this belief seems baseless. Some students, however, who bother themselves about most uninteresting things, have discovered the reason of the opinion. The secret is Aaron's face. With much practice, he has learned to wear a look of profound intelligence. The casual observer would really think he knows something. He usually finds a listener because it is interesting to watch him try to simulate sense. Aaron wants to become a teacher. He always has lots to do, and sometimes mentions casually that he teaches. I wonder what.

name: Veazey, George Ross
description: Now, mesdames and messieurs, the next physiognomy is one George Ross—no relation to Charlie. This blushing, buxom, clever, classical boy is remarkable for his unlimited powers of work. If there is anything to be done that requires real work, who is called upon but Georgie Porgie. He is most accommodating, and if you happen to be in the same class in Latin with him it is a waste of time and money to buy and use a crib, for he has all such publications beat a mile (for I have used many and am an authority). But, speaking of cribs, you ought to see this rosy boy tucked in his little bed! Turn the crank quick, Larry, he is disrobing.

name: Wallis, Benjamin Franklin
description: Don't be deceived, my dear people, in this innocent-looking, soft-voiced gentleman. He looks like he could not hurt a flea, but, as a matter of fact, he's the guy who broke the strength-testing machine up in Abber's gym. What's more than that, he almost won a half-mile race once; indeed, he would have won if there had not been three people in front of him. His track team training comes in well on the geological trips, for he's the only man who can keep up to Dr. Schwartz. That's going some. Slide them along, Larry.

name: Theodore S. Will
description: Ladies and gentlemen—to my left is "Ted, the famous ladykiller," the only one in captivity in the United States. He was captured on the corner of Howard and Lexington Streets, where he was setting the girls wild with his good looks (in his own imagination). When Ted is not idling away his time with his "calico" friends, he is nosing around the dusty and moldy corners of the different libraries of McCoy Hall, trying to find some work that has not yet been discovered by any other bookworm. Ted, without doubt, takes more interest in the University than any other student; why, he even was usher once at a debate. Ted is studying for the ministry; we all know what kind of a hot-air artist he is, so turn the crank, Larry.

name: Alan Churchill Woods
description: Finally, ladies and gentlemen, we will present to you a flattering portrait of the social star of our collection. Young Wutz, last of a long line, scion of a noble race. In his own opinion, he is the world with a fence around it; in ours, well, he's Wutz. He has strange habits. He sits smoking for hours, his eyes rolled heavenwards, thinking how great he is. He stands glowingly before the mirror admiring himself. As a beau he is unequaled; as a dilettante he is charming; as an actor he is unsurpassed. Nobody knows how different he can be—at times almost attractive and, really, quite intelligent; at other times foolish, or peevish, or wild. That's all, ladies and gentlemen. Larry, pack up those slides carefully and turn off the light. Good-bye, everybody, and come again.
